title: Quiz: Lattice Fundamentals
description: Test your understanding of lattice mathematics, bases, and their properties

questions.question: What is a lattice in the mathematical sense?

options.text: Any grid of equally-spaced points in space
A discrete additive subgroup of ℝⁿ generated by linearly independent vectors
A continuous function from ℝⁿ to ℝ
A matrix with integer entries

questions.explanation: A lattice is formally defined as a discrete additive subgroup of ℝⁿ, which can be characterized as the set of all integer linear combinations of n linearly independent vectors (the basis). This gives it both algebraic structure (group) and geometric structure (discrete).

questions.question: If B is a basis for lattice L, which matrix transformation produces another valid basis for the SAME lattice?

options.text: Multiply B by any invertible real matrix
Multiply B by a unimodular matrix (integer matrix with det = ±1)
Add any constant to all entries of B
Scale B by any nonzero real number

questions.explanation: A matrix U is unimodular if it has integer entries and det(U) = ±1. If B is a basis, then B' = B·U is another basis for the same lattice. This works because U maps integer coordinates to integer coordinates bijectively (both U and U⁻¹ have integer entries).

questions.question: What does the determinant of a lattice represent geometrically?

options.text: The number of lattice points
The length of the shortest vector
The volume of the fundamental domain (parallelepiped)
The dimension of the lattice

questions.explanation: The determinant det(L) = |det(B)| equals the volume of the fundamental parallelepiped. This is a lattice invariant—it doesn't change with different basis choices. It measures the 'density' of lattice points: smaller det means denser lattice.

questions.question: Given basis vectors b₁ = [3, 0] and b₂ = [1, 2], what is the lattice determinant?
questions.explanation: The determinant is |det(B)| = |det([[3,1],[0,2]])| = |3·2 - 0·1| = |6| = 6. This means the fundamental parallelepiped has area 6, and on average there's one lattice point per 6 square units.

questions.question: What is the first successive minimum λ₁(L) of a lattice?

options.text: The length of the first basis vector
The minimum distance between any two lattice points
The length of the shortest nonzero vector in the lattice
The determinant of the lattice

questions.explanation: λ₁(L) is the length of the shortest nonzero vector in the lattice. This is NOT necessarily a basis vector! Finding λ₁ is the Shortest Vector Problem (SVP), which is NP-hard. Note that the minimum distance between points equals λ₁ since the difference of any two lattice points is also a lattice point.

questions.question: The Hadamard ratio of a basis B is defined as det(L)/∏‖bᵢ‖. What does a Hadamard ratio close to 1 indicate?

options.text: The basis vectors are nearly parallel
The basis vectors are nearly orthogonal
The lattice has high dimension
The shortest vector is difficult to find

questions.explanation: For an orthogonal basis, det(L) = ∏‖bᵢ‖ (product of lengths), so the Hadamard ratio equals 1. As the basis becomes more skewed, the product of norms increases while the determinant stays constant, so the ratio decreases. A ratio close to 1 means nearly orthogonal basis vectors.

questions.question: What does the LLL algorithm achieve?

options.text: Finds the exact shortest vector in polynomial time
Produces a reduced basis with shorter, more orthogonal vectors
Computes the lattice determinant
Converts any matrix to a lattice basis

questions.explanation: The LLL (Lenstra-Lenstra-Lovász) algorithm is a polynomial-time lattice basis reduction algorithm. It doesn't find the shortest vector exactly, but produces a 'reduced' basis where vectors are relatively short and nearly orthogonal. The first vector of an LLL-reduced basis is within a factor of 2^((n-1)/2) of λ₁.

questions.question: According to Minkowski's theorem, what is the upper bound on λ₁ for an n-dimensional lattice with determinant 1?

options.text: λ₁ ≤ 1
λ₁ ≤ √n
λ₁ ≤ n
λ₁ ≤ 2ⁿ

questions.explanation: Minkowski's first theorem states λ₁(L) ≤ √n · det(L)^(1/n). For det(L) = 1, this gives λ₁ ≤ √n. This means every lattice must contain a nonzero vector of length at most √n times the n-th root of its determinant.

questions.question: The Shortest Vector Problem (SVP) has known polynomial-time quantum algorithms, similar to how Shor's algorithm breaks RSA.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: False! Unlike factoring (which Shor's algorithm solves efficiently), there are NO known polynomial-time quantum algorithms for SVP or other hard lattice problems. The best known quantum algorithms for SVP are still exponential time. This is precisely why lattice-based cryptography is considered quantum-resistant.
